prompt: Create a full budget baseline for a 12-month enterprise platform migration project with a 20-person cross-functional team (8 developers, 4 QA, 2 architects, 2 PMs, 2 DevOps, 1 UX, 1 BA). Include cost structure, bottom-up estimates, contingency reserves, and monthly cash flow.
assertions: produces a cost breakdown structure with categories for labor, infrastructure, licenses, travel, and contingency
maps cost accounts to WBS work packages with unique identifiers
calculates labor costs using role-specific fully-loaded rates for all 20 team members
itemizes non-labor costs including cloud infrastructure, software licenses, and tooling
calculates contingency reserve using at least one quantitative method (EMV, percentage, or Monte Carlo)
separates contingency reserve (identified risks) from management reserve (unknown unknowns)
produces a monthly cash flow projection across all 12 months
generates an S-curve showing cumulative planned expenditure over time

prompt: Build a budget baseline for a 10-month digital transformation project where 40% of the work is outsourced to external vendors (offshore development team, SaaS integrator, and UX agency). Internal team is 12 people.
assertions: distinguishes internal labor costs from external vendor/outsourcing costs
applies different rate structures for internal FTEs vs external contractors
accounts for vendor payment terms and milestone-based billing
models the 60/40 internal-external cost split with separate budget lines
includes vendor management overhead in indirect cost calculations
addresses currency or rate differential risks for offshore resources
produces cash flow that reflects vendor invoice timing vs internal payroll cycles

prompt: Create a budget baseline for a 6-sprint agile product development project (12 weeks) where scope is variable and the team commits to a fixed capacity of 8 developers and 2 QA. Use capacity-based budgeting.
assertions: adopts capacity-based budgeting rather than scope-based estimation
calculates budget based on team size and sprint duration rather than feature-level estimates
includes per-sprint cost breakdown showing fixed team cost per iteration
accounts for sprint ceremonies and non-delivery overhead in capacity calculations
addresses how scope variability affects budget utilization without changing the baseline
provides a mechanism for tracking budget consumption by epic or theme
includes guidance on when re-baselining is warranted in an agile context

prompt: The project scope has changed: a new regulatory compliance module has been added (+3 months duration, +5 FTEs for 4 months, +$200K in compliance tooling). Perform a budget re-baseline showing before/after comparison and the change control impact.
assertions: documents the scope change with traceability to change request or CR number
produces a before vs after budget comparison at the summary and category level
calculates the incremental cost of the new module (labor + non-labor + overhead)
updates the contingency reserve to reflect new risk profile from added scope
revises the monthly cash flow projection to incorporate the extended timeline
updates the S-curve with the new baseline overlaid against the original
includes a re-baseline approval workflow or recommendation for change control board
